## [00:00 - 03:01]

Now in this video, what I want to show you is what the next decisions are based on the data that we've got. So when I look at this set of campaigns, I'm looking for that stability as an indicator as to what to do next. Now number one, if I've got good stable results on my graduated campaigns and my ad sets, then what I'm going to do is take them back to phase two of graduation testing, which is now bringing in new ads. So for example, if I go into my lookalikes campaign, I have lookalike audiences there that are based on different pixel events. So 1% of initiate checkout, 1% of people that view the video, view content, add to car, etc. And what I can do at this stage is make some next decisions. So if I've tested these 1% and they've gone through the graduation testing process, so through phase one to see if the audience is good, phase two to find additional ads at work. But now I'm at phase three with these prospecting audiences. I know that they work. So looking at this data, I've spent, for example, one, two, three, four, three, three and a half K on these three ad sets. And I've maintained a good row ass. So that is far more confidence than I need, but it's enough confidence to know that those three ads, and you know, this one's there or thereabouts, as if your optimization tweaks still to be done. But these three ad sets, these audiences are working really well. So what do I do next? I then push the 2%, the 3%, the 4%, the 5% and up to 10% into my test phase. Because the way I look at lookalikes is you start off with a core audience. So for example, this is people that have initiated checkout. I believe in the last 90 days off the top of my head. So these are people that have initiated checkout. And I create the lookalike from that, that lookalike data seems to work. Now I want to take that back to phase two of my grad test, because I know that the 1% works, and test new ads, and then back to phase one of my grad test process, and test the additional lookalike. So there'll be a sheet on how to step through this in terms of the testing process. But that's one of the first decision points I want to build in. Because this plays a big part into optimization and growth. Because as you start to spend more money on your ads, especially for example, this is 1% in the US, a couple of million people. If I now scale this from spending 1K a week to 10K a week to 100K a week, I need bigger audiences, and that 1% will exhaust fairly quickly. But now if I've gone through the test process and I've introduced a 5%, a 10%, and they're also working with whichever ads are resonating,

## [03:01 - 06:07]

then I can build stability. Because as I scale up, I'm not worrying about my audiences are starting to fatigue. So that's one of the decision points. The other decision point that I would be looking at is, based on these particular 1% audiences. And now one thing you'll notice here, I have two ways of graduating my lookalikes. Sometimes what I'll do is push them straight into what I call a super lookalike. A super lookalike takes, if similar sized audiences and groups them together. So a super lookalike would have taken these three audiences, which are the three, the best audiences for me right now, and grouped them into one ad set. So that one ad set would have 1% initiative. So check out 1% of video views, 95%, 1% of view content in one ad set. Then what I would do is duplicate that ad set two or three times and let Facebook bounce between the ad sets in terms of spend. But the reason we haven't done this for right now is, because when we went through phase one of testing, the actual audiences were slightly different. So although these are all 1% audiences, the age range and the demographic for who we were targeting were slightly different. So although here we've got three 1% lookalike ad sets, and generally we would merge them into one, we can't do that right now because of the difference in performance for the age range and also the gender as well. So there's just something as a consideration. I always prefer fewer ad sets and kind of super lookalikes and stacked interests, for example. So if I've got five different interests at work, when I go to scale, I'd rather combine those five. But sometimes you do find that audiences work with different types of demographic. And that's really important because if you're testing one audience, a 1% lookalike, 3% lookalike, the seed audience, which means the custom audience, there could be a certain age range or gender that's in there, which then tells the lookalike what kind of other people do look for. But when you create the lookalike, you might find different age ranges perform better. So for example, in this initiate checkout custom audience, we targeted everyone that has initiated checkout. But when we went through testing, we found that the males 35 to 65 perform better than the females. And that specific age range. How did we do that? Because we went into our breakdown report in testing, and we looked at age and gender breakdown. So that gave us an idea of performance. This also leads on to another point is, now at this stage, what would I do to optimize and take that on? Well, actually, I can break that down into different segments to look at opportunities, which I'll show you in the next video.
